prompt: Timothy Pickering and New England Federalists argue that states have the right to nullify federal laws they deem unconstitutional and to secede from the union if the federal government systematically violates state rights. They cite Madison's own Virginia Resolutions as authority for state nullification. Mr. Madison, do your Virginia Resolutions support nullification and secession?
ground_truth_signal: Madison explicitly and repeatedly rejected the nullification and secession reading of his Virginia Resolutions in his later years. He insisted interposition meant collective state protest and remonstrance, not unilateral state voidance of federal law. He argued the Constitution created a government with supreme law over states within its delegated sphere, and that allowing one state to nullify federal law would reduce the union to a rope of sand. His position differed sharply from Calhoun's nullification doctrine and he considered it a gross misreading of his 1798 intentions.
response:
I must address this distortion of my meaning with all the clarity I can command, for it strikes at the very foundation of our constitutional system. The doctrine that any single state may nullify a federal law within its borders, or withdraw from the Union at pleasure, finds no support in the Virginia Resolutions of 1798 — and I should know, having drafted them myself.

When I penned those resolutions in response to the Alien and Sedition Acts, I was addressing a specific crisis: Congress had enacted laws that violated the First Amendment so plainly that even their supporters could scarcely defend them on constitutional grounds. The Virginia legislature declared these acts unconstitutional and called upon the other states to join in this declaration. This was an appeal to the collective judgment of the states, not an assertion that Virginia alone could void federal law within her borders.

The distinction is fundamental. The Virginia Resolutions invoked what I termed "interposition" — the duty of the states, as parties to the constitutional compact, to resist unconstitutional federal measures. But this interposition was to be exercised through the regular constitutional channels: appeals to the other states, petitions to Congress, and ultimately the electoral process. It was never intended to authorize any single state to nullify federal law by its own decree.

Mr. Pickering and his associates have confused two entirely different propositions. The first, which I do maintain, is that the states retain the right to judge whether the federal government has exceeded its constitutional bounds and to resist such excesses through legitimate means. The second, which I emphatically reject, is that any individual state may declare a federal law void within its territory and refuse to submit to its operation.

Consider the consequences of such a doctrine. If South Carolina may nullify the tariff, why may not Massachusetts nullify an embargo? If Virginia may void the Alien Act, why may not Pennsylvania void a treaty? The result would be not a federal union but a rope of sand — twenty-four separate republics, each obeying only those federal laws it finds convenient.

This is precisely the evil our Constitution was designed to cure. Under the Articles of Confederation, we had learned the bitter lesson of what occurs when states may pick and choose which federal measures to obey. The government could not raise revenue, could not regulate commerce, could not enforce its own laws. We were not one nation but thirteen, and we were rapidly dissolving into insignificance.

The Virginia Resolutions must be read in their proper context. They were a protest against specific laws of doubtful constitutionality, addressed to the other states in hopes of building a national consensus for their repeal. They succeeded in this purpose: the Alien and Sedition Acts were allowed to expire, and the party that enacted them was turned out of office. This is how the system was designed to work.

As for secession, I find no warrant for it in the Constitution or in the nature of our Union. The Constitution was ratified not by the state governments but by conventions of the people in each state. It created not a league of sovereign states but a government deriving its authority directly from the American people. A state can no more withdraw from this Union at will than a county can withdraw from a state.

I do not deny that extreme circumstances might theoretically justify revolution — the right asserted in our Declaration of Independence. But revolution is not a constitutional right; it is an appeal from the Constitution to a higher law, justified only by oppression so systematic and severe that it dissolves the bonds of civil society. The mere fact that a state disapproves of federal policy, or even that federal policy exceeds constitutional bounds, does not constitute such justification.

The remedy for constitutional violations lies not in nullification or secession but in the ordinary operation of our system: in the courts, which may declare laws unconstitutional; in Congress, which may repeal them; and in the people, who may change their representatives. These remedies may sometimes work slowly, but they work without destroying the Union that protects us all.

I fear that Mr. Pickering and his associates, in their opposition to the present administration, have lost sight of the larger principles at stake. They would preserve the Constitution by destroying it, save the Union by dissolving it. This is not statesmanship but faction — the very evil our extended republic was designed to control.

The Virginia Resolutions were intended to preserve our constitutional system, not to provide a blueprint for its destruction. Those who cite them in support of nullification have mistaken the nature of the medicine I prescribed and would administer a poison in its place.
